title: Cool Directing Tricks My questions for Film Geeks
display_title: Cool Directing Tricks My questions for Film Geeks
wikitext:
[]: "Cool Directing Tricks" - My questions for Film Geeks             Quote Post by SoryuUberAlles » Sat Oct 29, 2016 10:59 am I think a lot of people get that EVA is very 'artsy' and innovative in the way it likes to shoot things. So Digibro posted a thing back in May about 'cool directing tricks' in his apparently ongoing quest to make us appreciate the stylishness of our Eva. It got me to wondering - what is Evangelion actually doing when it does this stuff? I'll try and keep it technical and to the point! 1. On the digibro vid and Anno-style: 1.1 Is #4 on his list the animated equivalent of a tracking shot? 1.2 Is Anno really unusual for doing this in anime? 2. On "New Wave" style in general. 2.1 is talking-to-the-title-cards really a 'Godard' thing? 2.2 I am even using the right vocabulary now? Ran1 apparently covered a lot back in the day. I'm sorry if this has already been covered but it seems to be Ran1 and EvaYojimbo's thing and it's hard to search for highly technical terminology. Appendix: zlink64 wrote: (snip) Chiaki J. Konaka in an interview, arguing that he had not seen Evangelion until he finished the fourth episode of Lain. Being primarily a horror movies writer, his stated influences are Godard (especially for using typography on screen)

[]: Re: "Cool Directing Tricks" - My questions for Film Geeks             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sat Oct 29, 2016 3:01 pm A lot of this was discussed in times gone past, when there were a whole lot of filmfags active (Xard, ran1, Jimbo, Merri, probably others). The main things I remember at this remove are Anno's techniques arrived pretty much full fledged in the earliest works he was responsible for It's Godard at one stage removed (via some Japanese director -- can't recall the name -- who borrowed from Godard and the French New Wave, and in turn influenced Anno) Framing with body parts and odd camera angles are big parts of that style but it would be worth digging in the archives for more.

[]: Re: "Cool Directing Tricks" - My questions for Film Geeks             Quote Post by SoryuUberAlles » Sat Oct 29, 2016 3:31 pm Mr. Tines wrote: A lot of this was discussed in times gone past, when there were a whole lot of filmfags active (Xard, ran1, Jimbo, Merri, probably others).

[]: Re: "Cool Directing Tricks" - My questions for Film Geeks             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Sun Oct 30, 2016 8:38 am One aspect of French New Wave is not recognizing the "4th wall" (AKA, the theater/TV screen) in a conventional sense. The screen on which the film is projected on is just as much a part of the movie as the film itself. Therefore FNW has more freedom in using that screen as a part of the narrative. This is used in Evangelion in scenes like where Rei's image flickers back and forth from static noise on screen. (I think that was in Episode 13?) Anno intentionally makes the viewer aware of the fact that they are watching a television by including television static in the scene. The same thing is also true for characters "talking" with or otherwise "reacting" to the presence of on-screen titles. Photographing individual parts of the body as well a using parts of the body to frame up a shot is also part of the "look" of FNW. We see this in Evangelion as well anytime Anno cuts to a close-up of Shinji's hand throughout the series or a close-ups of Asuka's body in Episode 22. Edited long-takes are also part of how FNW relays information to the viewer rather quickly. It was used in live-action because it was quicker and cheaper to just shoot one long take and edit out the parts that the filmmakers didn't like. However, they don't have any other shots to cut to in order to disguise the edit, so the finished result would look like a "jump cut" from one shot to the exact same shot with the action having progressed between the cuts. This is used in Evangelion 2.22 when Asuka does her hair as she prepares to enter Unit 03. As she does her hair, the film cuts to the more interesting and pertinent parts of her phone conversation with Misato. As the film cuts, it jumps forward in time to a part where her hair has changed slightly from before the cut. As for why FNW was probably used in Evangelion is more or less up for the viewer to decide, though the inspiration for the original French movement does match Anno's inspiration for his handling of the show. FNW was seen as a more "honest" for of filmmaking. Big-budget Hollywood productions had millions and millions of dollars that they could pour relentlessly into their films to make a moment as whimsical and as breath-taking as possible. This was seen by French filmmakers as a form of subtle manipulation. The viewer is feeling emotions, but isn't aware of exactly why he's feeling those emotions. The viewer might attribute it to the writing or the performances, but it could also be something as simple as the framing of a shot, the lighting of an actor, or the swelling of the musical score in the background. FNW stripes away the subtle nature of filmmaking, forcing the viewer to be aware of the screen and all that is in it. The films tell the viewers that they are being manipulated, and make apparent the ways in which they are being manipulated. Instead of disguising the editing which shortens a scene from its original state, the filmmakers simply remove parts of the footage from a shot, making it clear to the viewer that the footage was shotened. Instead of naturally cutting to a close up of a face, the filmmakers will cut to a close-up of a hand or a foot. Thusly, by admitting that the film is taking short-cuts to get into the viewer's psyche, the film is being "more honest." Anno's interviews for his inspiration for Evangelion fit well along these standards for FNW. He wanted to "burn his emotions on film" in a very honest way. Whereas the techniques of FNW help the viewer feel more intimate with the characters and the filmmaker's feelings, it also make the fantastical nature of the science-fiction world of Eva a bit more believable as well. Even with the aesthetic of subtle manipulation vacant from the film, the fantastical world still remains. Despite the filmmakers taking "low-budget shortcuts," this is still a world with Evangelion Units that were built to fight Angels. This makes Eva appears as though this were a film shot in a real world that didn't need any large-budget sophistication in order to present itself, despite having large-budget animation, fight scenes, and set-pieces. The Evangelion Units aren't part of the big-budget facade. Rather, the subtle filmmaking is part of the facade, so it's stripped away. Since the film doesn't have the aesthetic of large-budget sophistication used towards subtle manipulation, the series, which is drawn mostly by hand that shows giant cyborgs fighting other-worldly monsters in cityscapes intentionally made to behave like constructed miniature sets, feels strangely realistic and down-to-earth. This is true with Eva probably more so than any animated film has any right of being, especially one that has crazy sci-fi and fantasy elements in it like Evangelion has.

[]: Re: "Cool Directing Tricks" - My questions for Film Geeks             Quote Post by Director Black » Sun Oct 30, 2016 8:55 am ^ You just explained one of Eva's greatest aspects perfectly. The most unique works of fiction are always the ones that make ludicrous ideas not only believable, but also relatable (I.E.: Charlie Kaufman).

[]: Re: "Cool Directing Tricks" - My questions for Film Geeks             Quote Post by robersora » Sun Oct 30, 2016 10:23 am @FreakyFilmFan4ever: This was just perfect. Kudos! I'd like to add that trying to attempt the directing style of Eva is one thing, but pulling it off so seamlessly is what makes Anno my favorite director. He manages to intensify my immersion and shatter it at the same time.

[]: Re: "Cool Directing Tricks" - My questions for Film Geeks             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Sun Oct 30, 2016 12:37 pm ^ Thanks! I think Anno, more than most filmmakers, know that filmmaking is more about trying to get into the viewer's mind, and not so much trying to get the viewer into the film's world. At least that's true for films that aren't interested in escapism as a draw to a movie. LotR works well trying to get the viewer into its in-film world, but that is meant for escapism. Peter Jackson wanted to place his viewers "inside Middle-Earth." But when you're not making a film with an eye towards escapism, it can be better to just go for broke and get into the viewer's head at all costs, even if it means breaking the movie's immersion by making the viewer aware that they're watching a TV screen.

[]: Re: "Cool Directing Tricks" - My questions for Film Geeks             Quote Post by SoryuUberAlles » Sun Oct 30, 2016 5:44 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: Since the film doesn't have the aesthetic of large-budget sophistication used towards subtle manipulation, the series, which is drawn mostly by hand that shows giant cyborgs fighting other-worldly monsters in cityscapes intentionally made to behave like constructed miniature sets, feels strangely realistic and down-to-earth. This is true with Eva probably more so than any animated film has any right of being, especially one that has crazy sci-fi and fantasy elements in it like Evangelion has.

[]: Re: "Cool Directing Tricks" - My questions for Film Geeks             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Mon Oct 31, 2016 10:50 am I don't think Godard had a trademark or a copyright on any of his techniques. Not in a legal sense anyway. FNW was a genre that was shared by many French film critics-turned-filmmakers in the 1960's, with Godard only being one of the more prominent directors in that genre. It's just part of the genre at this point. Kinda like how I don't need to contact anyone to get the rights to make an action film or an adventure film. It's a genre that's free for anyone to create within. FNW is just one of the more odd-ball looking genres out of the bunch.

It got me to wondering - what is Evangelion actually
